planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; Fortune's holly fern is more cold-hardy than the Japanese holly fern (C. falcatum) and performs well in zone 6b–9.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; Fortune's holly fern is a cold-hardier alternative to the commonly planted Japanese holly fern (C. falcatum), with similarly glossy, leathery, dark green pinnate fronds that maintain their attractive appearance year-round. The fronds are somewhat more finely textured and slightly smaller than those of C. falcatum. Like its relative, it is exceptionally shade tolerant and adaptable to dry shade conditions once established — a combination of traits that makes it one of the most reliable evergreen ground cover ferns for the difficult conditions of dry shade under trees. In zone 9a, it performs beautifully as a long-lived, low-maintenance evergreen element in the shade border, maintaining good appearance through heat and dry spells better than many woodland ferns.

common_name: Bird's Nest Fern (Japanese Bird's Nest Fern)

planting.note: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic, moist soil in partial to full shade; Japanese bird's nest fern is more cold-tolerant than the tropical bird's nest fern (A. nidus) and suitable for zones 8–11 outdoors. In zone 9 it grows well as a shade garden perennial in sheltered areas.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic, consistently moist soil in deep to partial shade; Japanese bird's nest fern is a larger, more temperate species than the commonly sold tropical bird's nest fern (A. nidus), with long, strap-like, undivided fronds that radiate from a central rosette in a vase or nest-like arrangement — the origin of the common name. The fronds are glossy, bright green, and somewhat wavy, creating a bold, architectural statement in the shade garden. Unlike the deeply tropical A. nidus, this Japanese species tolerates cooler temperatures and is adaptable for outdoor growing in zone 8–11 gardens. It grows as an epiphyte on tree trunks in its native habitat but adapts well to moist, well-drained soil in the shade garden. A striking focal plant for moist shade borders in zone 9a where its bold, undivided fronds provide contrast with textured ferns.

planting.note: Divide established rhizomatous colonies in spring or plant container stock in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to deep shade; Japanese bamboo fern spreads slowly by underground rhizomes to form a colony in appropriate moist, shaded conditions.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to deep shade; Japanese bamboo fern is a distinctive fern with unusually large, pinnate fronds where the pinnae (leaflets) are broad and undivided — resembling bamboo leaves more than typical fern fronds. The fronds are held on long, arching stems, creating an elegant, flowing effect quite unlike the lacy texture of most ferns. New growth in spring emerges in shades of yellow-green to lime green before maturing to medium green. The plant spreads slowly by rhizomes to form a graceful colony in moist shade. In zone 9, it provides a bold, unique foliage texture in the shade border — different from all other commonly available shade plants. The cultivar 'Flavomaculata' has yellow blotches along the midrib of each pinna, creating a variegated effect.

planting.note: Plant container stock or divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, limestone-derived soil in partial to deep shade; spider brake is a limestone-loving fern that self-sows into cracks in walls, sidewalks, and stone paving in the humid South.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, limestone-derived or mortar-rich soil in partial to deep shade; spider brake is a delicate, lacy fern from eastern Asia that has naturalized extensively in the humid American South, particularly in shaded walls, the cracks of old brick buildings, mortar joints, and limestone outcrops — wherever moisture collects in alkaline, calcium-rich substrates. The narrow, ribbon-like pinnae arranged around the rachis give the frond an elegant, spider-like appearance that makes it ornamentally distinctive. It is one of the few ferns that prefers alkaline conditions, making it valuable for shade gardens with limestone-derived soils or on the north-facing walls of masonry structures. An excellent fern for naturalizing in the mortar joints of shaded stone walls and pathways in zone 9a.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring as new fronds emerge, or plant container stock in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to deep shade; Japanese painted fern is one of the most popular and widely grown ornamental ferns for temperate shade gardens.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to deep shade; Japanese painted fern is one of the most visually striking ferns for the shade garden, with fronds that combine silver, gray, green, and burgundy-red in complex, painterly patterns — the silvery overlay on the frond surface creates a shimmering, metallic effect unique among temperate ferns. The red to burgundy midrib (rachis) and stipe contrast dramatically with the silver-gray frond surface. Numerous cultivars vary in the intensity and proportion of silver and red — 'Pictum' (silvery), 'Ursula's Red' (deep burgundy), and 'Silver Falls' (cascading silver) are particularly popular. Grows best in cool-summer climates and may decline in the heat and humidity of the Gulf South, making it more suitable for zone 9 gardeners in the Upper South or with summer shade and consistent moisture.
